title: Bought Quiet
passage:
When the fencing-style sport of tavro introduced an automated touch-sensor to replace its judges' eyes, the officials' guild braced for a flood of overturned decisions. The flood never came. Over a full season the sensor reversed barely one call in thirty — the human judges, it turned out, had been reading the fast exchanges better than anyone had credited. What the system changed was not the scoreboard but the room. Bouts that once stalled while fencers protested now ran on without argument, because a light, not a person, had spoken; crowds settled faster, and matches finished on time. The guild's senior official, Denez Aru, is not sure the trade is a clean one. "We bought quiet," he says, "and I am not certain quiet is the same thing as fairness."
— Sabine Wold, sports desk

questions.prompt: What does the text imply was the main effect of introducing the touch-sensor?

options.text: Its main effect was to reverse barely one disputed call in every thirty across the whole of the season.
Its main effect fell on the conduct and pace of matches rather than on the number of decisions that came out differently.
It showed that the sport's human judges had in fact been getting most of their fast-exchange calls wrong.
It proved that automated officiating will inevitably replace human judges across other similar sports before long.

questions.rationale: Family ELF-TYPE-002 (inference / implication), short text. STEM-LAW: the stem asks for the implied main effect but does not name whether it was on the scoreboard or on the room. WHY-KEY (B): the passage states 'What the system changed was not the scoreboard but the room. Bouts that once stalled while fencers protested now ran on without argument ... crowds settled faster, and matches finished on time.' B infers that the chief effect was behavioural (conduct and pace) rather than corrective, exactly one inch beyond the stated contrast. WHY-DISTRACTORS: A = too_literal — the one-in-thirty figure is true, but the passage frames it as what did NOT change much ('The flood never came'), so it describes the sensor's minor corrective effect, not the main effect the stem asks for; tempting because it is a precise, real number. C = quantifier_upgrade / contradiction — inverts the finding that the judges 'had been reading the fast exchanges better than anyone had credited' into their getting 'most of their ... calls wrong'; tempting because it reuses the fast-exchange vocabulary. D = outside_knowledge / too_far — a sweeping prediction about other sports the text never makes; Aru's doubt ('I am not certain quiet is the same thing as fairness') in fact pulls against any triumphal reading; tempting to a reader who supplies a familiar technology-replaces-humans narrative.

generator_meta.topic: sports officiating technology: an automated touch-sensor in a fictional fencing-style sport reverses few calls but changes match conduct and pace, raising a quiet-versus-fairness doubt

fictional_source.byline: Sabine Wold, invented sports journalist; official Denez Aru invented

fictional_entities: the officials' guild and its senior official Denez Aru (invented; all quotes invented)
generator_meta.spelling_note: BrE held throughout; no AmE-only spellings present.
generator_meta.engineered_traps: too_literal at A (the real one-in-thirty figure, which the passage marks as the effect that did NOT arrive), contradiction/quantifier_upgrade at C (judges 'better than credited' -> 'getting most calls wrong'), outside_knowledge/too_far prediction at D; key B is the balanced inference and is not the sole hedged option, since A is also stated without absolutes.
